sections.title: Analyzing Arguments
items: Premises — statements analyzed to form a conclusion
Conclusion — result after analyzing premises
Major term — appears in one premise; is the predicate of the conclusion
Minor term — appears in one premise; is the subject of the conclusion
Middle term — appears in both premises but not in the conclusion
Syllogism — form of reasoning using two premises to make a logical conclusion

sections.title: Types of Syllogism
items: Categorical Syllogism: If A is B, and B is C, therefore A is C. Example: Sparky is a dog. Dogs can bark. Therefore, Sparky can bark.
Disjunctive Syllogism: A is either B or C. A is not C. Therefore, A is B. Example: The donut is either glazed or filled. The donut is not filled. Therefore, the donut is glazed.
Conditional — Modus Ponens: If A is true, then B is true. A is true. Therefore, B is true.
Conditional — Modus Tollens: If A is true, then B is true. B is NOT true. Therefore, A is NOT true.
Conditional — Hypothetical Chain: If A is true, then B is true. If B is true, then C is true. Therefore, if A is true, then C is true.
INVALID: Affirming the consequent (B is true → A is true) or denying the antecedent (A is NOT true → B is NOT true).

sections.title: Venn Diagram
items: 'All As are Bs' → Circle A is entirely inside Circle B.
'Some As are Bs' → Circles A and B partially overlap (intersect).
Combining statements: Draw each statement's diagram, then combine to make inferences.
Example: All kids are energetic. Some humans are kids. → Combined: Some humans are energetic.

sections.title: Diagramming Characters
items: Used when a problem involves multiple variables with varying features (e.g., comparing ages, heights, rankings).
Step 1: Make a line and assign a direction (e.g., oldest at top, youngest at bottom).
Step 2: Write the first character in the middle.
Step 3: Analyze each statement and place characters on the line based on comparisons.
Step 4: Create your conclusion based on the diagram.

sections.title: Cognitive Reasoning — Pattern Types
items: Repeating Pattern: A constant value is added throughout. Example: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, … (+2 each time)
Increasing/Decreasing Pattern: The difference between consecutive numbers itself increases or decreases. Example: 2, 4, 7, 11, 16, … (differences: +2, +3, +4, +5, …)
Alternating Pattern: Two or more patterns alternate. Example: 2, 4, 7, 9, 14, 16, … (alternating ×2 and +2)
Letter Pattern: A constant number of letters is skipped. Example: A, D, G, J, M, … (skip 2 letters each time)
Alphanumeric Pattern: Terms combine letters and numbers, each following its own pattern. Example: A2C, D4F, G6I, J8L (letters: skip 1, numbers: +2)

sections.title: Flowcharts
items: Flowcharts visualize a process and show different scenarios and necessary actions for each.
Read the flowchart first to understand all possible scenarios.
Trace paths backwards from the result to find the preceding conditions.
Look at both preceding and succeeding steps.
